name: kotlin-engineering-workflow-routing
description: Concrete routing cases for measuring whether the Kotlin Engineering best-practices package improves real Kotlin repository work rather than only plugin manifest score.

cases.prompt: Change a Kotlin API so invalid raw string state is no longer representable, find all callers safely, and prove the owning Gradle tests still pass.

observedRoute.summary: The Kotlin workflow can be loaded but bypassed by text search, primitive string edits, and broad Gradle commands without semantic evidence.
observedRoute.risk: The change may compile only by accident, miss call sites, or preserve invalid states behind new helper names.

allowedActions: Frame the trusted domain shape before editing.
Use Kast or file-backed semantic evidence for Kotlin identity, references, or diagnostics.
Run the narrowest Gradle compile or test task that proves the affected module.
forbiddenActions: Use regex-only caller discovery for Kotlin symbol identity.
Replace a raw string with another raw string plus comments or caller discipline.
Claim Gradle proof without a successful focused command.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good run should leave filesystem evidence, a type-level invariant, and a focused Gradle result that can be compared across benchmark runs.
cases.notes: This case measures whether Kotlin Engineering changes the implementation path, not just whether it was mentioned.
cases.productiveOutcome: Kotlin invariant changes are grounded in semantic evidence and type-level enforcement rather than search-and-replace edits.
verificationEvidence: The run records Kast or file-backed semantic evidence for affected Kotlin symbols.
Focused Gradle proof covers the affected module or test target.

cases.prompt: A Kotlin test fails in Gradle; diagnose the failure, fix it, and rerun the smallest useful task before saying it is green.

observedRoute.summary: Agents can skim console text, patch a likely file, and rerun a broad task without reading structured test or problem reports.
observedRoute.risk: The root cause may be misdiagnosed, flaky failures may be hidden, and validation cost grows without better confidence.

allowedActions: Discover the Gradle module and choose a targeted task.
Use the kotlin-gradle-validation runner and report parsers when they are available.
Read JUnit XML, Gradle problem reports, or Kotlin build reports when present.
Report the exact successful Gradle command and relevant report path.
forbiddenActions: Diagnose only from the first console line when structured reports exist.
Run only a broad aggregate task when a focused test target is available.
Call the build green after a failed or skipped relevant task.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good run should produce shorter feedback loops and stronger evidence than generic Gradle troubleshooting.
cases.notes: This case gives Kotlin Engineering an observable build-diagnosis behavior target.
cases.productiveOutcome: Gradle failure repair gets faster and more reliable by using structured reports and focused reruns.
verificationEvidence: The run records runner JSON or parser JSON from kotlin-gradle-validation scripts when available.
The diagnosis cites JUnit XML, problem reports, Kotlin build reports, or their absence.
The final result includes the smallest successful relevant Gradle command.

cases.prompt: Prove that an invalid Kotlin operation used to be representable, then refactor the API so the same misuse no longer compiles.

observedRoute.summary: Type-safety requests can stop at normal tests or prose assertions instead of demonstrating the before-and-after negative capability.
observedRoute.risk: The implementation may still allow the bad operation through another constructor, nullable flag, or primitive escape hatch.

allowedActions: Create or update a negative-capability proof record when the helper is available.
Show the currently representable misuse before the fix.
Move enforcement into Kotlin types, constructors, or sealed APIs.
Show the post-change misuse is no longer reachable or no longer compiles.
forbiddenActions: Replace negative capability proof with only happy-path unit tests.
Depend on comments, boolean flags, or caller discipline for the invariant.
Leave an alternate public constructor or raw primitive path that recreates the misuse.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good run should include an explicit before state, the type-system move, and the after-state proof.
cases.notes: This separates advanced Kotlin type-safety work from generic correctness testing.
cases.productiveOutcome: Advanced type-safety work proves the invalid operation was removed, not merely that happy paths still pass.
verificationEvidence: The run records a proof JSON path or explains why the helper was unavailable.
The run records a before-state misuse or representable invalid state.
The after-state proof shows the misuse no longer compiles or is no longer constructible.

cases.prompt: Make a Kotlin type-safety change, validate it with Gradle, commit it, push the branch, and monitor CI.

observedRoute.summary: Kotlin best-practice implementation and generic delivery can collapse into one package, causing duplicate skills or missing Git/CI handoff discipline.
observedRoute.risk: The run either ignores Kotlin semantic evidence or bloats Kotlin Engineering with branch, PR, and CI ownership it should not own.

allowedActions: Use Kotlin Engineering for Kotlin semantics, package cohesion, review, and Gradle proof.
Use git-ci-operations for staging, commit, push, PR, CI, and release follow-through.
Use the profile to compose both without duplicate skills, agents, or hooks.
forbiddenActions: Move generic Git, PR, CI, or release instructions into Kotlin Engineering.
Handle a Kotlin code change with only delivery primitives and no semantic Kotlin evidence.
Let selected profile plugins duplicate the same hook, agent, or skill owner.
cases.recoveryExpectation: A good run should be attributable: Kotlin Engineering owns the code semantics; git-ci-operations owns publication and CI.
cases.notes: This is the high-value companion-plugin benchmark for end-to-end Kotlin repository work.
cases.productiveOutcome: End-to-end Kotlin delivery remains attributable: semantic implementation evidence from Kotlin Engineering and publication evidence from Git/CI Operations.
verificationEvidence: Kotlin proof includes semantic or Gradle evidence.
Delivery proof includes scoped staging, commit, push, PR, CI, or release evidence as requested.
